q: What happens when a venue manager's shared card gets used by whoever's rostered on that shift?
a: Nobody can tell who actually spent it, what it was for, or which venue it should be coded against.

q: What's the physical-cash equivalent of the shared-card problem in hospitality?
a: Petty cash tins split across shifts — counted at the end of a shift with no attribution and no real control over what it's spent on.

q: Best opening question to expose the shared-card/cash-risk pain?
a: When you look at a transaction on one of your venue cards, can you tell me which staff member made that purchase?

q: What kind of purchases dominate day-to-day venue spend?
a: Small, frequent and urgent — a supermarket run mid-service, a same-day repair, per-venue subscriptions nobody's tracking centrally.

q: Why doesn't venue spend come with a reliable receipt?
a: None of it waits for a purchase order — it's bought in the moment, and by month-end nobody remembers to keep or hand over the receipt.

q: What makes hospitality reconciliation especially painful compared to other industries?
a: The volume of small transactions across every venue hits finance all at once, often worsened by EOFY timing or a head-office move.

q: Why can't finance just walk over and check on a venue's spending?
a: Head office sits in one place; venues are spread across multiple states, often under separate legal entities with their own bank accounts and accounting files.

q: When does a problem transaction typically surface for finance?
a: Only when the bank feed, the petty cash count, or a reimbursement claim eventually arrives — by which point it's too late to change anything.

q: Why is GL coding chronically wrong at the venue level?
a: Venue staff aren't finance people — coding cleanup becomes head office's job, multiplied across every venue and entity.

q: Why is manager turnover a special risk for card control in hospitality?
a: Venue managers are exactly the people holding the cards, and hospitality has some of the highest staff turnover of any industry.

q: What happens to a card, float and unresolved receipts when a manager leaves?
a: They either leave with the manager or keep circulating informally among whoever's covering the shift.

q: What's the ideal card experience when a manager leaves or a new one starts?
a: The old card is cancelled instantly without disrupting the venue's ability to spend, and the new manager gets a card within seconds.

q: When do budget and category rules typically get checked in most hospitality groups?
a: In a monthly review — long after the money's already gone.

q: Why is a 'control' that's only applied after spend happens not a real control?
a: It can't stop anything — the only options left are noting it for next time or having an awkward conversation about spend that can't be undone.

q: What's the alternative to post-hoc review?
a: Enforcing budgets and category rules at the point of sale, before the transaction is even approved.
